subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Fast Radio Bursts
description: A quick, practical guide to Fast Radio Bursts.

hook.text: Fast radio bursts are millisecond flashes of radio waves from billions of light-years away. Nobody knows exactly what causes them.

buildup.text: The first was found in 2007 by Duncan Lorimer in archival data. Hundreds have been detected since, mostly one-time events.

discovery.text: In 2020, a magnetar in the Milky Way produced an FRB-like burst—the first time a source was identified for these signals.

twist.text: Some FRBs repeat. FRB 20121102A fires hundreds of bursts, suggesting its source survives each explosion.

climax.text: CHIME telescope in Canada detects multiple FRBs daily. The mystery is shrinking, but surprises keep coming.

punchline.text: Millisecond flashes from billions of light-years away. Magnetars are the prime suspect.

quiz.question: What is the leading candidate for the source of fast radio bursts?
options: Magnetars—neutron stars with extremely powerful magnetic fields
Colliding black holes at the centers of distant galaxies
Alien civilizations sending interstellar communication signals
